sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa dell'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente. Il conflitto tra Israele e Iran continua a pesare sui mercati, con il Brent che ha raggiunto $101.02 (-2.06%) e il VIX che si attesta a 26.07 (-4.12%). La Fed e altre banche centrali stanno monitorando la situazione, ma non hanno ancora preso misure drastiche. BlackRock Investment Institute e Goldman Sachs Insights hanno pubblicato report settimanali che sottolineano la necessità di cautela.
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off due to rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. The conflict between Israel and Iran continues to weigh on markets, with Brent reaching $101.02 (-2.06%) and VIX at 26.07 (-4.12%). The Fed and other central banks are monitoring the situation, but have not yet taken drastic measures. BlackRock Investment Institute and Goldman Sachs Insights have published weekly reports emphasizing the need for caution.

market_impact_summary.it: Il mercato azionario è sotto pressione, con S&P 500 a 6632.19 (-0.61%) e STOXX 600 a 59.13 (-0.55%). Il dollaro si indebolisce leggermente, con EUR/USD a 1.14 (-0.76%) e DXY a 100.26 (-0.10%). Il mercato obbligazionario è in tensione, con TLT a 86.54 (-0.49%) e US 10Y Yield a 4.28% (+0.28%). Il prezzo dell'oro scende a $4997.40/oz (-1.09%).
market_impact_summary.en: The stock market is under pressure, with S&P 500 at 6632.19 (-0.61%) and STOXX 600 at 59.13 (-0.55%). The dollar weakens slightly, with EUR/USD at 1.14 (-0.76%) and DXY at 100.26 (-0.10%). The bond market is tense, with TLT at 86.54 (-0.49%) and US 10Y Yield at 4.28% (+0.28%). Gold price drops to $4997.40/oz (-1.09%).

audio_script_it:
Benvenuti al consueto briefing mattutino dei mercati. 
  Oggi, 16 marzo 2026, il sentiment di mercato è decisamente risk-off, con un punteggio di 7 su 10. 
  Questo è principalmente dovuto all'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente, 
  dove il conflitto tra Israele e Iran continua a pesare sui mercati.

  I dati chiave di questa mattina sono:
  - Il prezzo del greggio Brent che ha raggiunto $101.02, in calo del 2.06%,
  - L'indice Vix che si attesta a 26.07, in calo del 4.12%,
  - Lo Standard and Poor's 500 che ha ceduto lo 0.61% portandosi a quota 6632 punti.

  Passiamo ora all'analisi dei mercati asset per asset. 
  Il cambio euro dollaro si attesta a 1.14, in calo dello 0.76%, 
  mentre l'indice del dollaro (DXY) è a 100.26, in calo dello 0.10%. 
  Questo indica una leggera debolezza del dollaro rispetto all'euro.

  L'ETF obbligazionario Treasury (TLT) è a 86.54, in calo dello 0.49%, 
  e il rendimento del decennale americano è a 4.28%, in aumento dello 0.28%. 
  Questo mostra una certa avversione al rischio da parte degli investitori.

  L'oro si attesta a $4997.40/oz, in calo dell'1.09%, 
  mentre Bitcoin guadagna l'1.22% e si attesta intorno ai settantamila dollari.

  L'indice Stoxx seicento europeo è a 59.13, in calo dello 0.55%, 
  mentre l'indice Nikkei giapponese è a 53,751, in calo dello 0.13%. 
  Anche lo Shanghai Composite è in calo dello 0.26% a 4084.79.

  Sul fronte geopolitico, il conflitto tra Israele e Iran continua a dominare la scena. 
  La situazione è tesa e potrebbe avere ulteriori ripercussioni sui mercati.

  La Federal Reserve, la Banca Centrale Europea e altre banche centrali stanno monitorando la situazione, 
  ma non hanno ancora preso misure drastiche. 
  Tuttavia, è probabile che le banche centrali mantengano una posizione cauta nei prossimi mesi.

  Il team di ricerca di BlackRock Investment Institute ha pubblicato un report settimanale 
  che sottolinea la necessità di cautela in questo momento. 
  Anche gli strategist di Goldman Sachs hanno espresso preoccupazioni simili.

  Passando ai dati macroeconomici, l'ultimo dato disponibile sul CPI statunitense, 
  rilasciato il 1 febbraio scorso, mostrava un aumento del 2.7% su base annua. 
  Il dato sul Core CPI era anch'esso al 2.7%. 
  Il mercato del lavoro statunitense ha registrato una sorpresa negativa con -92K di Non-Farm Payrolls.

  La BCE e la Fed saranno sotto i riflettori questa settimana, 
  con le loro decisioni sui tassi di interesse che potrebbero influenzare i mercati.

  Il presidente degli Stati Uniti, Donald Trump, ha lanciato un avvertimento agli alleati europei 
  in merito alla questione di Nato e alla crisi nel Golfo Persico.

  La crescita economica cinese sembra essersi ripresa, 
  ma Pechino avverte di rischi geopolitici.

  Il primo ministro del Regno Unito, Sir Keir Starmer, 
  presenterà un pacchetto di sostegno energetico da £50 milioni 
  per alleviare l'impatto della crisi.

  Questa settimana sarà cruciale per capire come le banche centrali 
  intenderanno rispondere alla crisi geopolitica e ai suoi effetti sull'economia.

  Per quanto riguarda i dati specifici da monitorare, 
  segnaliamo la prossima uscita del PCE Core YoY il 28 marzo 
  e il GDP QoQ statunitense previsto per il 29 aprile.

  In conclusione, il sentiment risk-off continua a dominare i mercati, 
  con l'incertezza geopolitica come principale fattore di rischio. 
  Sarà importante monitorare le mosse delle banche centrali e i dati macroeconomici 
  per capire come evolverà la situazione.
audio_script_en:
Welcome to your daily morning market briefing.

Market sentiment is risk-off today, scoring 7 out of 10, as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East continue to escalate. The ongoing conflict between Israel and Iran is weighing heavily on markets. This has led to a rise in safe-haven assets and a decline in riskier ones. Key data points include Brent crude oil reaching $101.02, down 2.06% today, and the Vix index at 26.07, down 4.12%. These movements reflect the growing uncertainty and caution among investors.

Let's take a look at how different assets are performing today. The euro-dollar exchange rate is at 1.14, down 0.76%. The dollar index is at 100.26, down 0.10%. The Standard and Poor's 500 is at 6632.19, down 0.61%. The Vix index, as mentioned, is at 26.07, down 4.12%. The Treasury bond ETF, which tracks 20-year US Treasury bonds, is at 86.54, down 0.49%. The 10-year US Treasury yield is at 4.28%, up 0.28%. Gold is at $4997.40 per ounce, down 1.09%. Bitcoin is at $73,678, up 1.22%. The Stoxx six hundred, a European stock index, is at 59.13, down 0.55%. The Nikkei, a Japanese stock index, is at 53,751, down 0.13%. The Shanghai index is at 4084.79, down 0.26%.

In terms of geopolitics, the situation in the Middle East continues to deteriorate. The conflict between Israel and Iran is having a direct impact on oil prices and investor sentiment. There are concerns about the potential for this conflict to spread and disrupt global trade and stability. 

On the macroeconomic front, the Federal Reserve and other central banks are closely monitoring the situation. While they have not yet taken drastic measures, there is a clear emphasis on caution. The BlackRock Investment Institute and Goldman Sachs Insights have both published reports highlighting the need for investors to be cautious given the current geopolitical risks.

The last available US Consumer Price Index data, from February, showed a year-over-year increase of 2.7%, slightly down from 2.8% previously. The core CPI, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, was also at 2.7%, down from 2.9%. Non-farm payrolls showed a decrease of 92,000 jobs, and the unemployment rate was at 4.4%, up from 4.3%. These data points are a bit stale, having been released 43 days ago, but they provide some context for the current economic situation.

Looking ahead, investors are eagerly awaiting the release of the Personal Consumption Expenditures Core data, which is due on March 28. This data will provide more recent insights into inflation trends. The Federal Reserve's current funds rate is at 3.64%, and there is speculation about potential future adjustments in response to both the geopolitical situation and economic data.

Global M2 liquidity, a proxy for global money supply, is expanding and currently stands at $22.4 trillion, with a year-over-year increase of 4.3%.

In news today, former US President Trump has warned European allies about NATO, in the context of security concerns in the Hormuz region. There are also concerns about the potential for the Iran conflict to reawaken global inflation fears. China's economy has shown signs of rebounding, ahead of the Iran crisis. Central banks around the world are setting interest rates, with a focus on guidance amid the current uncertainty. 

The UK's Labour Party leader, Starmer, is set to announce a £50 million energy support package to ease the fallout from the Iran conflict. 

From a geopolitical perspective, there are several key issues. The Arctic region's security is becoming increasingly important due to climate change. The global impact of the new Middle East war is being closely watched. A Japanese leader is planning a trip to Washington, amid efforts to address the Middle East shock. There are concerns about escalation or potential off-ramps in the Gulf, as well as the situation in Lebanon. The war in Sudan continues, with deepening regional rivalries. 

In conclusion, it's a cautious start to the day, with investors closely watching geopolitical developments and macroeconomic data releases. Key data to watch in the coming days includes the PCE Core data on March 28 and the US GDP data on April 29. These will provide important insights into the state of the economy and potential policy responses.

That's all for now. Thank you for joining us for today's financial update.

He also talks to Crisis Group’s Lebanon expert David Wood about the latest Israel-Hezbollah war.

articles.snippet: Yet the Iran crisis may push Gulf rivals to set aside their differences and revive stalled diplomacy on Sudan.

articles.title: The Danger of Vibe Patriotism in Defense Tech
articles.snippet: At conferences, in pitch decks, and increasingly in public writing, “service” is increasingly being used to describe the work of startup founders, employees, and venture capital investors in defense technology. The claim, sometimes explicit, more often implied, is that building defense technology is itself a form of service comparable to uniformed military service.It is visible in corporate messaging that invites civilians to “serve” through product work, from Palantir’s “Why We Serve” to Anduril’s description of “national service as a form of innovation,” to public-private innovation narratives that describe entrepreneurship as a form of national service and “answering the call” advocacy The post The Danger of Vibe Patriotism in Defense Tech appeared first on War on the Rocks .

articles.title: Sinopec Slashes Refining Runs as Hormuz Disruption Squeezes Crude Supply
articles.snippet: Sinopec, China’s biggest oil refiner, has reduced its run rates by 10%, Bloomberg reported today, citing unnamed sources, in response to the supply squeeze resulting from the traffic disruption in the Strait of Hormuz. The size of the cut is equal to about half a million barrels daily. There will also be additional output losses from maintenance operations, the sources said. The refining major accounts for about a third of China’s total refined petroleum product output, with an average processing rate of 5.2 million barrels daily, the…

articles.title: Middle East Crisis Could Triple Pakistan’s Oil Import Bill
articles.snippet: Previously, we reported that Pakistan could find itself sucked into the Middle East war thanks to the Strategic Mutual Defence Agreement (SMDA) it signed with Saudi Arabia in September 2025. The core of the agreement states that any aggression against one country is considered an aggression against both, potentially requiring Pakistan to intervene against Iran’s missile strikes on Saudi Arabia. However, military confrontation with its southerly neighbor might turn out to be a less existential threat for Pakistan. Surging oil prices are already…

articles.title: How the Iran War Could Reshape Global Energy Policy
articles.snippet: The U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran and the ongoing conflict in the Middle East have caused the biggest oil disruption in history. The closure of a major transit route between Asia and Europe, as well as restrictions on fossil fuel production in the region, have driven oil and gas prices up, a trend that is set to continue. So, as governments are facing rising consumer energy bills once again, will it be enough to encourage a Covid-era pivot to renewable energy? The U.S. war against Iran has caused the largest oil disruption in history, according to…

articles.title: Oil Prices Remain Volatile as Hormuz Tensions Rise
articles.snippet: Oil markets remained volatile in early Asian trade on Monday as Trump attempts to form a broader coalition of countries to protect shipping through the Strait of Hormuz amid fresh attacks on energy infrastructure across the Middle East. At the time of writing, West Texas Intermediate had dipped 0.53% to $98.19 after an initial surge, while Brent was continuing its climb, rising 0.41% to $103.60 per barrel. The volatility follows a weekend of escalation in the Middle East conflict, beginning on Friday evening when the U.S. bombed military assets…

articles.title: EU Ministers Race to Shield Industry From an Escalating Energy Shock
articles.snippet: The European Union is in a rush to find ways to alleviate the energy cost pain for consumers and businesses, with energy ministers of member states set to meet later today to discuss options. These, according to Reuters, include boosting the supply of carbon permits to reduce their price, state support for industrial energy consumers, and tax cuts. The reportedly discussed options suggest European leaders are making a rare move to prioritize energy affordability over emission reduction. The option with greater availability of carbon permits is…
